note: The character of a statutory power a holder may exercise (Annex A6.3). Active from Spiral 2. v0.2 (2026-07-20) added definitions; v0.3 (2026-07-20) added `adjudication` and `application_to_court`; v0.4 (2026-07-21, sponsor decision) adds `compulsory_acquisition`, `consent_order` and `guidance` — best-practice alignment from a taxonomy scan (OPC Common Legislative Solutions; LRRA 2006 s.32; Planning Act 2008 s.33; the LKIF/Francesconi Provision Model), NOT invention. Also sharpens the consent/approval boundary and adds the `group` facet.
group_facet: Each term carries a `group`: legislative | administrative | adjudicative — the one power-classification with canonical UK pedigree (de Smith -> Wade & Forsyth -> Craig). It is a DRILL-DOWN grouping over the leaf terms (for the card/map), flagged as an analytic frame, NOT a rule of law. Legislative = norm-setting; administrative = executive-discretionary; adjudicative = judicial/quasi-judicial.
attributes_not_types: Some distinctions are ATTRIBUTES on a record, not new terms: `amends_primary` (a Henry VIII rulemaking power — TNA 'power to amend/modify/repeal conferred'); a sanction sub-type (RES Act 2008 Part 3's closed civil-sanctions list); instrument form (OPC's regulations/order/rules/byelaw). Do not split these into power_type terms.
judicial_layer: RESOLVED (sponsor, 2026-07-20). 'other' had been carrying 6 of 20 powers in the Water tranche, including the two most load-bearing records in the special-administration trace - the Secretary of State's petition to the High Court (WIA 1991 s.24(1)(a)) and the Court's own order-making power (s.24(1)). Two families had no term and are now added. They are deliberately kept SEPARATE rather than collapsed into a single 'judicial' term, because they are opposite ends of the same interaction: one actor ASKS a court, another IS the court and decides. Merging them would erase exactly the distinction the delivery chain depends on. The catch-all sense of 'judicial' belongs on the FUNCTIONAL axis instead - a property of the body (see vocab/functions.json `judicial`), which is what a query or a map colour should key on.
typing_rule: Choose the term describing what the holder DOES with the power, not the policy area it operates in. Where two terms fit, prefer the more specific. 'other' requires a `notes` entry saying why no term fits.

terms.definition: Power to make rules of general application - regulations, orders, licence conditions, codes, standards, determinations binding a class. Carry `amends_primary: true` where it can amend primary legislation (a Henry VIII power).
Power to confer, vary or withdraw a legal status on a person, body, site or thing.

Power to issue statutory guidance or a code of practice that others must follow or 'have regard to'. A soft but real blocker: statutory guidance a body must have regard to constrains its lawful choices, and departure invites challenge. Anchored in LRRA 2006 s.32(a) ('giving guidance'), the Regulators' Code and OPC drafting practice.
terms.test: Statute empowers the holder to issue guidance / a code / a statement of principles that a named actor must follow or have regard to.

terms.definition: Power to require another body or person to act, or not act, in a specified way, binding on the recipient.
Power to agree or refuse agreement to what another actor PLANS to do - a PROSPECTIVE acceptance, sought before the act. Contrast `approval`. Usually generates a derived Veto.
terms.test: 'may, with the consent of X' / 'X may not do Y without the consent of Z' - the act has not yet happened; the holder gates it in advance.

terms.definition: Power to approve or withhold approval of something ALREADY done or completed - a RETROSPECTIVE acknowledgement of and agreement to a completed decision, scheme, plan or set of rules before it takes effect. Contrast `consent` (prospective).
terms.test: 'subject to the approval of X' / 'X must approve the scheme' - a completed thing (a plan, a scheme, a set of rules) is put to the holder for sign-off.

terms.definition: Power to make a bespoke statutory ORDER that authorises infrastructure works AND bundles/displaces the other consents those works would otherwise need. Materially changes the veto map: it REMOVES vetoes that would otherwise exist. Anchored in Planning Act 2008 s.33 (a DCO bars the ordinary consent regimes and bundles them). Examples: the DCO (Planning Act s.114), Transport and Works Act orders (s.1/s.3), harbour revision/empowerment orders (Harbours Act s.14).
terms.test: A single order that consents to works AND disapplies/removes other consent requirements for them. Carry `displaces_consents` / `bundles` where modelled.

terms.definition: Power to grant, refuse, vary, attach conditions to or revoke an authorisation without which an activity may not lawfully be carried on.

Power to authorise the compulsory purchase or acquisition (or temporary possession) of land or rights in land, overriding the owner's consent. A coercive power central to 'who can block this?' because it OVERRIDES private-property vetoes. Anchored in the Planning Act 2008, TWA 1992, Harbours Act 1964 and the Acquisition of Land Act 1981.
terms.test: Power to take land / rights compulsorily (CPO, compulsory acquisition, temporary possession). The blocked party is typically a private landowner (the #29 state->private view).

terms.definition: Power to appoint, reappoint or remove a person to or from an office or body.
Power to provide financial support - grants, loans, indemnities, guarantees. Frequently gated by a Treasury consent, which is its own derived Veto.
Power to levy fees, charges or levies to fund functions.
Power to require the provision of information, documents or explanations.
Power to enter, examine or survey premises, records or activities. (Coercive entry/search/seizure is a candidate sub-type - flag if it recurs.)
Power to open and conduct a formal inquiry into a matter, person or body.
Power to compel compliance with a legal requirement - enforcement notices, orders, undertakings, injunctive steps. Distinguished from `sanction` by aiming at compliance rather than punishment.

Power to impose a penalty for non-compliance - financial penalties, censure, licence revocation as punishment. Distinguished from `enforcement` by aiming at punishment. Carry a sanction sub-type (RES Act 2008 Part 3) as an attribute, not new terms.

Power of a court or tribunal to determine a matter and make a binding order - to grant, refuse, quash or vary. The holder DECIDES; it is not a party. Example: the High Court's power to make a special administration order under WIA 1991 s.24(1).

Power to hear and determine an appeal against another actor's decision. This is the power of the appellate BODY; the appellant's right of appeal is what makes the original decision `overridable`. Where the appellate body is a court, prefer `adjudication`.

Power to apply or petition a court for an order. The holder ASKS; the court decides, and may refuse. Distinguished from `adjudication` by which end of the interaction the holder sits at. Example: the SoS's petition for a special administration order (WIA 1991 s.24(1)(a)). Normally record the deciding court in `related_body_ids`.
A statutory power whose character matches none of the above. Requires a `notes` entry saying why. A recurring 'other' is a vocabulary gap to be raised, not absorbed.
